title: Module 1 quiz: Postgres is the platform

questions.prompt: Why Postgres beats NoSQL for most app workloads in 2026?

choices.text: ACID transactions across multiple rows/tables, native joins, strong typing with JSONB for flexibility, mature query planner with EXPLAIN, and a rich extension ecosystem (PostGIS, pgvector, pg_trgm). Modern Postgres handles tens of thousands of writes/sec on a primary; replicas and sharding scale further. NoSQL still wins for massive horizontal write scale or pure key-value workloads, but most B2C/B2B SaaS, e-commerce, and marketplace apps are fundamentally relational.

questions.explanation: Relational guarantees + JSONB + extensions fit most app workloads.

questions.prompt: Choosing primary keys for user-facing tables. Recommendation?

choices.text: UUIDs via `gen_random_uuid()` (pgcrypto). Generated client-side so no server round-trip to know the ID; no information leakage (incrementing integers expose row counts); distributed-friendly with no collision risk across regions or shards; compatible with Supabase Auth's `auth.users.id` type. Exception: high-volume internal tables where bigint sequential IDs save storage — but for user-facing data, UUIDs are the right default.

questions.explanation: UUIDs are the right default for user-facing tables.

questions.prompt: What does PostgREST give you for free?

choices.text: A REST API generated from your Postgres schema — every table becomes an endpoint, every column queryable. SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE with rich filter operators (eq, gt, in, contains, etc.), foreign-key-based embedding for joins in one request, upsert, RPC for SQL functions. Authentication via JWT; authorization enforced by RLS policies. For ~80% of CRUD operations, you don't write API code.

questions.explanation: Auto-generated CRUD REST from schema; RLS handles authz.

questions.prompt: Need posts with authors and comments-with-authors in one request. How?

choices.text: Use PostgREST embedded resources via foreign-key traversal: `.select('id, title, author:author_id(id, username), comments(id, body, author:author_id(username))')`. One HTTP request, one SQL query underneath. Solves N+1 cleanly. Aliases like `author:author_id` rename embedded fields; nested embedding works recursively as long as foreign keys are defined.

questions.explanation: Embedded resources via FK traversal in one `.select()`.

questions.prompt: Adding a NOT NULL column to a 5M-row table safely?

choices.text: ALTER TABLE ADD NOT NULL in one shot.

Three-step migration: (1) add the column nullable, (2) backfill in batches (1k-10k row chunks via loop or pg_cron — avoids whole-table locks), (3) ALTER to NOT NULL once backfilled. Pair with `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` for any new index. The pattern: additive first, fill safely, tighten constraints last. Test on a staging branch database before prod.

questions.explanation: Add nullable → batched backfill → set NOT NULL. CONCURRENTLY for indexes.
